title: US and Iran Agree 60-Day Roadmap in Switzerland; Iran to Readmit IAEA Inspectors
summary: The first high-level US-Iran negotiations since the June 17 ceasefire MoU concluded in Bürgenstock, Switzerland, with mediators Qatar and Pakistan announcing a joint 60-day roadmap toward a final deal. Three technical working groups were established covering nuclear issues, sanctions, and dispute resolution. Iran's agreement to allow IAEA inspectors back into key facilities is the first concrete concession since the ceasefire took effect, with US Vice President JD Vance calling it a 'very, very good' first round of talks.

coverages.headline: US-Iran roadmap: Final deal, Lebanon deconfliction at Switzerland talks
coverages.excerpt: Mediators Qatar and Pakistan announced a joint 60-day roadmap toward a final deal, creation of nuclear and sanctions working groups, and Iran agreeing to allow IAEA inspectors to return — the first concrete concession since the ceasefire MoU was signed June 17.
coverages.bias_notes: CNBC's framing treats this primarily through a markets-and-diplomacy lens appropriate to its outlet identity, correctly highlighting the IAEA inspection return as the first concrete, verifiable concession rather than just a diplomatic statement — a meaningful distinction, since pledges and statements have been common throughout this conflict while actual verifiable actions have been rare. The dual Qatar-Pakistan mediator role is reported neutrally, but for an Indian readership this detail carries real significance: it's the same Pakistan mediator dynamic that Indian commentary elsewhere has described as causing diplomatic 'heartburn' in New Delhi, since Pakistan's profile as a credible international broker is rising at exactly the moment India's own foreign policy is focused on isolating it post-Sindoor. This piece, written for a US audience, has no reason to flag that tension — but Indian readers should hold the two facts together.
